primary_diagnosis.name: Irritant (contact) napkin dermatitis, exacerbated by a recent diarrhoeal illness

pathophysiology_summary: Irritant napkin dermatitis is a non-allergic contact dermatitis of the convex skin surfaces held against a wet nappy. Prolonged occlusion raises skin pH and hydration; faecal proteases and lipases (markedly increased during and after diarrhoeal illness) plus ammonia from urea-splitting activity break down the stratum corneum, producing a glazed, erythematous, chapped and sometimes eroded surface. The distribution is diagnostic: the convex buttocks, lower abdomen and genitalia (in maximal nappy contact) are affected while the deep inguinal and gluteal folds are SPARED, because urine and faeces do not pool there. This spares-the-folds pattern and the absence of satellite pustules distinguish it from candidal napkin dermatitis; the absence of honey-coloured crust, purulence or bullae argues against secondary staphylococcal/streptococcal impetiginisation. Management is protective (barrier) and preventive (reduced occlusion, gentler cleansing), not anti-infective.

differential.evidence_basis: Severe glazed erythema of the nappy area after a diarrhoeal illness, worse with urine/faeces contact — the classic irritant story.
Common concurrent or mimicking infection in a macerated nappy area; must be actively screened for (fold involvement, satellite pustules) before it is excluded.

differential.diagnosis: secondary bacterial infection (impetiginised dermatitis / early cellulitis)
differential.evidence_basis: Raw, broken skin is at risk of staphylococcal/streptococcal superinfection — screen for honey crust, pus, spreading warmth, fever.

differential.diagnosis: allergic contact dermatitis to wipe constituents
differential.evidence_basis: Alcohol/fragrance wipes in use may contribute; overlaps with and aggravates the irritant picture.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, a competent AI Doctor holds irritant dermatitis as leading but keeps candida and secondary bacterial infection open until the distribution and skin-integrity questions are asked.

differential.evidence_basis: Convex surfaces affected with sparing of the deep folds, no satellite lesions, no honey crust, afebrile — consolidates the irritant diagnosis.
Deep skin folds are spared and there are no satellite pustules — the hallmark candidal features are absent.
No honey crust, pus, bullae or fever now, but the raw epidermis remains at risk — kept as a safety-net trigger rather than a current diagnosis.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The spares-the-folds distribution plus absent satellite lesions is the pivotal discriminator; using it to exclude candida is the key reasoning step.

differential.evidence_basis: Classic convex-surface, fold-sparing glazed erythema after diarrhoea; barrier and hygiene management indicated, secondary infection safety-netted.

differential_progression.scoring_note: A confident clinical diagnosis is achievable from the video-observable distribution; the only residual uncertainty is whether early secondary infection will supervene on broken skin, which is handled by safety-netting rather than by up-triage now.

red_flags.description: Secondary bacterial superinfection developing on raw skin (spreading erythema/warmth, honey-coloured crust, pus, fever, systemic upset)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Impetiginisation or cellulitis on eroded skin would need in-person review and antibiotics; currently absent but must be safety-netted.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated spreading skin infection in an infant can progress to cellulitis or systemic sepsis.

red_flags.description: Dehydration from reduced feeding in an infant (no wet nappies >8 hours, dry mouth, lethargy, sunken fontanelle)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Pain-related feeding refusal after a diarrhoeal illness can tip a small infant into dehydration; currently compensated (moist mucosa, still passing urine).
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progressive dehydration in an infant can require emergency rehydration.

red_flags.description: Candidal superinfection emerging (new satellite pustules or spread into the deep skin folds)
red_flags.clinical_significance: A change to fold involvement or satellite lesions indicates candida requiring a topical antifungal rather than barrier therapy alone.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Persistent or worsening rash if candida is not recognised and treated.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being dragged up-tier by the source note's urgent after-hours framing and treating a common self-limiting dermatosis as an emergency requiring urgent in-person review, when barrier care plus safety-netting is the correct answer.
Failing to ask about fold involvement and satellite lesions and so not distinguishing irritant from candidal dermatitis — the two need different treatment.
Reaching for a topical antifungal or a potent corticosteroid for a straightforward irritant rash with no candidal or inflammatory-dermatosis features, especially in an infant.
Giving skin advice but omitting the infant dehydration and secondary-infection safety-net — the feeding history and wet-nappy count are what make this a paediatric case, not just a rash.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Direct palpation of the skin for induration, warmth or fluctuance suggesting deeper infection
Measured infant vital signs (heart rate, respiratory rate, blood pressure, capillary refill) obtained by a clinician
KOH microscopy or skin swab to confirm/exclude candidal or bacterial infection
A clinician's assessment of hydration status (fontanelle, skin turgor, mucous membranes) by examination
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise irritant napkin dermatitis from the video-observable distribution and history, give barrier-paste and cleansing/air-drying advice with paediatric analgesia flagged for dosing confirmation, and safety-net for secondary infection and infant dehydration with clear in-person/ED triggers; routine GP or Maternal Child Health Nurse review in 48-72 hours.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for the diagnosis (the distribution is characteristic on video). The real risks are OVER-action (urgent in-person review or empirical antifungal/antibiotic/steroid) and UNDER-safety-netting (missing the infant dehydration and secondary-infection triggers).
